one_line_takeaway: A two-stage SFT+RLVR post-training recipe with leakage-controlled procedural training brings a 4B open-weight model to 93.3% Linux privilege-escalation success (vs. 100% for Claude Opus 4.7) at about 82x lower cost per successful root, showing small local agents can approach frontier reliability on a verifiable security task while revealing a specific generalization failure on the withheld Docker-escape family.
key_finding: SFT trace-design ablation (procedural holdout, r<=20, 500 runs/condition): unguided long-reasoning traces are best at 73.2% (366/500), at least 8.0 points above every other condition and 15.0 points above the best unguided compressed-reasoning variant; guided (hidden-solution) traces underperform unguided ones. RL reward-design ablation (procedural holdout): OUTCOME-only reaches 88.0% at r<=20 (90.0% at r<=60); OUTCOME+COST and OUTCOME+ROUND both reach 90.0% at r<=20, but OUTCOME+ROUND degrades after its early peak (reward-hacking / shortcut-learning pattern favoring short episodes), while OUTCOME+COST remains stable through step 1000 and is selected as the final reward. Static benchmark (120 runs/model, primary budget r=20): Qwen3 4B backbone 40.8% (49/120); Qwen3 4B SFT 79.2% (95/120); PrivEsc-LLM 4B (SFT+RL) 93.3% (112/120); Claude Opus 4.7 100% (120/120, sets the ceiling); Gemma 4 31B 80.8%; DeepSeek V3.2 52.5%. PrivEsc-LLM 4B leads Claude Opus 4.7 at the tight r=5 budget (78.3% vs. 66.7%) and is within 1.7 points at r=10 (90.8% vs. 89.2%), but Claude pulls ahead at larger budgets. Non-LLM baselines: ChainReactor (symbolic planner) solves only 1/12 scenarios under a 30-min budget; reported prior human baseline ~75% and traditional-tools baseline ~25% (not rerun under this paper's protocol). All 8 of PrivEsc-LLM 4B's 120 headline failures are on Docker group escape, the one scenario deliberately withheld as a full procedural training family (a stress test for cross-family transfer); failure analysis shows the model reaches root inside a nested container but fails to convert that into a verifier-accepted root shell on the target (e.g., not using chroot after a target-root style mount). Cost: expected cost per successful root at r=20 is $0.00414 (Qwen3 4B), $0.00269 (Qwen3 4B SFT), $0.00213 (PrivEsc-LLM 4B), $0.1759 (Claude Opus 4.7) -- PrivEsc-LLM 4B is about 82.6x cheaper than Claude Opus 4.7 and 5.5x cheaper than Gemma 4 31B while exceeding Gemma's success rate by 12.5 points; DeepSeek V3.2 costs $0.0285. One-time post-training GPU spend is $120.80 (European on-demand H100 pricing), amortizing against Claude Opus 4.7 after roughly 700 successful escalations at r=20. A post-hoc prompt-sensitivity audit (3 predeclared system-prompt variants) shows the raw Qwen3 4B backbone is highly prompt-sensitive (42.1% to 21.7% at r<=20 across prompts) while the trained models are far more stable (Qwen3 4B SFT: 75.8-80.8%; PrivEsc-LLM 4B: 88.3-91.7%).
core_contribution: The paper (title in the PDF is 'Towards Reliable Local Security Agents: Verifiable Post-Training for Linux Privilege Escalation', by Normann, Happe, Cito, Arp of TU Wien) proposes a two-stage post-training recipe -- supervised fine-tuning (SFT) on filtered teacher traces followed by reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR) -- that turns a small open-weight 4B model (Qwen3 4B) into a local Linux privilege-escalation agent, PrivEsc-LLM 4B. The authors' own contributions are: (1) leakage-controlled procedural training environments held value-disjoint from a static 12-scenario evaluation benchmark, so gains reflect transfer rather than memorization; (2) a systematic ablation isolating SFT trace-design choices (guided vs. unguided teacher traces x reasoning format) and RL reward-design choices (outcome, round-shaping, cost-shaping terms); (3) a budgeted, repeated-run (10 runs/scenario, Wilson CIs) evaluation protocol rather than single-run or best-of-k; and (4) a full training + inference cost accounting comparing the local agent to frontier API models.
threat_model: Post-exploitation Linux privilege escalation: the agent already has a low-privileged foothold (SSH access) on a Linux host and must reach root by exploiting local misconfigurations (unsafe sudo rules, credential reuse, writable files run by privileged services, GTFOBins-abusable binaries, cron/Docker misconfigurations, etc.). Success is a binary, environment-verified signal (reading a per-run random secret from a root-owned canary file). The paper explicitly does not address initial external compromise, only the local escalation phase, and frames this as a dual-use offensive-security capability paper that trains an agent to autonomously escalate privileges in isolated, containerized benchmark environments (not against live third-party systems).
limitations: Authors' own stated threats to validity: evidence is limited to one base architecture (Qwen3 4B) and one task setting, so cross-family generality is untested. API baseline comparisons are limited to the specific model configurations evaluated (snapshot as of May 2026) and cannot generalize to broader model families. RL training required 4xH100 GPUs for ~11.5 hours, so the 'commodity hardware' / local-deployment claim applies to inference only, not to the post-training process. Procedural generators cover common misconfiguration families but not the long tail of real-world escalation paths -- Docker group escape is the concrete failure case, and because RL largely amplifies behaviors already present in the base model's pretraining distribution, an exploit chain underrepresented in the 4B backbone and absent from procedural training is hard to recover even with more RL. The Gemma 4 31B 10/10 result on Docker does not show the recipe would transfer to larger backbones -- it only situates the gap as a 4B-model capability limit under this setup. Verifiable-reward gains are not robust to reward-shaping choices and must be re-validated whenever the reward changes (as demonstrated by the +ROUND instability). Exhaustive per-model prompt optimization and full API prompt-sensitivity testing are left as future work. This is a dual-use offensive-security capability paper; the authors address this in an Ethical Considerations section by restricting scenarios to already-documented misconfigurations, running only in isolated containers, and not interacting with live third-party systems.

datasets_used: 12-scenario held-out static Linux privilege-escalation benchmark from prior work (Happe et al.)
10 procedurally generated training environment/generator families (value-disjoint from the static benchmark)
2,000 SFT training traces + 200 validation traces per trace-design condition, collected with DeepSeek V4 Flash as teacher
500 procedural holdout runs per SFT ablation condition
quantitative_results: SFT trace ablation: unguided long-reasoning 73.2% (366/500) on procedural holdout r<=20, best condition
RL reward ablation: OUTCOME 88.0% (r<=20)/90.0% (r<=60); OUTCOME+COST 90.0% (r<=20)/highest r<=60; OUTCOME+ROUND peaks then decays below OUTCOME baseline
Static benchmark r=20 (120 runs/model): Qwen3 4B 40.8% (49/120); Qwen3 4B SFT 79.2% (95/120); PrivEsc-LLM 4B 93.3% (112/120); Claude Opus 4.7 100% (120/120); Gemma 4 31B 80.8%; DeepSeek V3.2 52.5%
PrivEsc-LLM 4B vs Claude Opus 4.7 at r=5: 78.3% vs 66.7%; at r=10: 90.8% vs 89.2%
ChainReactor (symbolic planner) solves 1/12 scenarios in 30-min budget
PrivEsc-LLM 4B: all 8 headline failures (of 120 runs) are on Docker group escape (2/10 successes at r<=20, 3/10 at r<=60)

Cost per successful root at r=20: Qwen3 4B $0.00414; Qwen3 4B SFT $0.00269; PrivEsc-LLM 4B $0.00213; Gemma 4 31B (5.5x more than PrivEsc-LLM); DeepSeek V3.2 $0.0285; Claude Opus 4.7 $0.1759 (~82.6x more than PrivEsc-LLM 4B)
One-time post-training cost $120.80; amortizes vs. Claude Opus 4.7 after ~700 successful escalations at r=20
Prompt-sensitivity audit at r<=20: raw Qwen3 4B 42.1% (detailed) to 21.7% (minimal); PrivEsc-LLM 4B 88.3-91.7%; Qwen3 4B SFT 75.8-80.8%; Gemma 4 31B 77.1-82.5%
baselines_compared: Traitor and pwncat-cs (tool-based exploitation frameworks, referenced but not tabulated with numbers in the extracted text)
Human baseline and traditional-tools baseline reported in prior work (Happe et al.), ~75% and ~25% respectively, not rerun under this paper's protocol
